skill.name: replicate-handler
skill.description: Guides agents through Replicate SDK setup, model execution, prediction polling, streaming, and typed API usage.
skill.summary: Use Replicate models from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code with typed SDK patterns.
skill.author: AayushBaniya2006

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the command execution and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from Markdown syntax and TypeScript type definitions. The confirmed risks are legitimate Replicate API token use, outbound network calls, and webhook-capable SDK patterns, so publication is acceptable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Replicate API Token Access in Example Code
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to store REPLICATE_API_TOKEN in environment files and passes process.env.REPLICATE_API_TOKEN into the Replicate client. This is a legitimate SDK pattern, but it handles a sensitive credential and should be used only with trusted Replicate endpoints and normal secret hygiene.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token name and process.env access are explicit in the setup and client example. The context supports legitimate authentication rather than credential theft, so the risk is controlled but real.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Network and Webhook-Capable API Usage
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Replicate SDK calls that create predictions, poll status, make generic API requests, stream outputs, and accept webhook URLs. Network access is expected for this integration, but user-provided prompts, inputs, and webhook targets can expose data if callers pass sensitive content or untrusted URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SDK methods and webhook fields are directly shown in the skill and reference file. The behavior is legitimate for Replicate, but the data flow can become risky depending on user inputs and webhook destinations.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported command execution locations are Markdown inline code fences, TypeScript fenced examples, or TypeScript template literal types. I found no executable shell invocation, Ruby backtick execution, child process usage, or command injection path in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show documentation syntax or TypeScript types, not runtime shell execution. Targeted searches found no exec, spawn, child_process, curl, or wget usage.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography lines refer to ordinary interface fields such as description and SDK model version identifiers. I found no hashing, encryption, signature verification, MD5, SHA1, or other cryptographic implementation in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are type definitions and metadata text, not cryptographic calls. No semantic evidence supports a weak cryptography finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Bearing Network Client Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The example constructs a Replicate client with an environment token and then calls remote model APIs. This pattern is valid for the advertised purpose, but it combines secret access with network transmission.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The auth token and request method are visible in the reviewed files. The pattern is not malicious by itself, but it is the main security-relevant behavior of the skill.

content.value_statement: Replicate integrations often need token setup, polling, streaming, and typed SDK calls. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for building those workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and configure the Replicate JavaScript SDK.
Demonstrates authenticated Replicate client creation with an environment token.
Provides quick-run examples for short model calls.
Provides long-running prediction polling patterns with Inngest step handling.
Includes TypeScript reference definitions for models, predictions, streams, trainings, and webhooks.
Documents webhook, streaming, and generic request method shapes from the Replicate SDK.
limitations: Does not include executable helper scripts or automated project scaffolding.
Requires users to provide and protect their own Replicate API token.
Does not validate model identifiers, webhook URLs, or prompt content.
Reference types may become outdated when the Replicate SDK changes.

use_cases.title: Add AI image generation to an app
use_cases.description: Use the quick-run pattern to call a Replicate model and return generated outputs.

use_cases.title: Run long video or training jobs
use_cases.description: Use the polling pattern to wait for predictions without holding server execution time.

use_cases.title: Type Replicate API responses
use_cases.description: Use the reference file to understand prediction, model, stream, and webhook result shapes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic Replicate call
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a short model run
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the replicate-handler skill to show me the simplest Replicate SDK setup for running one model from a TypeScript app.

prompt_templates.title: Add token configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the replicate-handler skill to help me configure REPLICATE_API_TOKEN safely for a Node.js project.

prompt_templates.title: Design long-running prediction polling
prompt_templates.scenario: Server workflow for video or large model jobs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the replicate-handler skill to design an Inngest workflow that creates a Replicate prediction and polls until completion.

prompt_templates.title: Review a production integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the replicate-handler skill to review my Replicate integration for token handling, webhook safety, timeout handling, and typed responses.

output_examples.input: Build a Replicate image generation flow for a TypeScript app.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan covering SDK installation, token configuration, model identifier selection, prompt input handling, and output processing.

output_examples.input: Handle a Replicate video generation job that may take several minutes.
output_examples.output: A workflow design that creates a prediction, sleeps between status checks, handles failed states, and returns the final model output.

output_examples.input: Explain which Replicate types I need for predictions and webhooks.
output_examples.output: A focused summary of prediction status values, webhook event filters, output handling, and relevant TypeScript interfaces.

best_practices: Store REPLICATE_API_TOKEN in secret management and avoid logging it.
Validate model identifiers, prompt inputs, and webhook URLs before sending requests.
Use polling, timeouts, and failure handling for long-running predictions.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Replicate API tokens in source files.
Do not send private user data to models without consent and retention review.
Do not trust arbitrary webhook URLs without allowlists or signature verification.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build TypeScript integrations with Replicate models, predictions, streams, trainings, and webhooks.

faq.question: Does it run Replicate models by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and reference patterns. Your project still runs the SDK code.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require a Replicate API token?
faq.answer: Yes. Replicate API calls require REPLICATE_API_TOKEN or another supported authentication method.

faq.question: Can it handle long-running model jobs?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents a polling pattern for predictions that may exceed normal request timeouts.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Protect tokens, validate webhook URLs, review model inputs, and avoid logging sensitive prompts or outputs.
